content:
# Ruth Chapter 1 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 880-881 | **Source Image:** RUT_001_page_001.jpg, RUT_001_page_002.jpg

---

**1** In the days when the judges judged, there was a famine in the land. And a man from Bethlehem of Judah went to sojourn in the fields of Moab -- he and his wife and his two sons.

**2** The name of the man was Elimelech, the name of his wife was Naomi, and the names of his two sons were Mahlon and Chilion -- Ephrathites from Bethlehem of Judah. They came to the fields of Moab and settled there.

**3** Then Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died, and she was left with her two sons.

**4** They took for themselves Moabite wives; the name of the one was Orpah, and the name of the other was Ruth. They lived there about ten years.

**5** Then both Mahlon and Chilion also died, and the woman was left without her two children and without her husband.

**6** She arose, she and her daughters-in-law, and set out to return from the fields of Moab, for she had heard in Moab that the LORD had attended to his people and given them bread.

**7** She went out from the place where she had been, and her two daughters-in-law were with her. They set out on the road to return to the land of Judah.

**8** And Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, "Go, return, each of you to the house of her mother. May the LORD deal with you in faithful love, as you have dealt with the dead and with me.

**9** May the LORD grant to you that you find rest, each in the house of her husband." She kissed them, and they lifted up their voices and wept.

**10** They said to her, "No -- we will return with you to your people."

**11** But Naomi said, "Turn back, my daughters. Why would you go with me? Do I yet have sons in my womb who could become husbands for you?

**12** Turn back, my daughters, go -- for I am too old to belong to a husband. Even if I said, 'I have hope,' even if I were to belong to a husband tonight and even bore sons --

**13** would you wait for them until they were grown? Would you shut yourselves away, refusing to belong to a husband? No, my daughters, for it is far more bitter for me than for you, because the hand of the LORD has gone out against me."

**14** They lifted up their voices and wept again. Orpah kissed her mother-in-law, but Ruth clung to her.

**15** And she said, "Look, your sister-in-law has turned back to her people and to her gods. Turn back after your sister-in-law."

**16** But Ruth said, "Do not urge me to leave you or to turn back from following you. For where you go, I will go, and where you lodge, I will lodge. Your people shall be my people, and your God shall be my God.

**17** Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. May the LORD do thus to me, and more besides, if anything but death parts me from you."

**18** When Naomi saw that Ruth was determined to go with her, she ceased speaking to her.

**19** And the two of them went on until they came to Bethlehem. When they arrived in Bethlehem, the whole city was stirred on account of them, and the women said, "Is this Naomi?"

**20** She said to them, "Do not call me Naomi; call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.

**21** I went out full, and the LORD has brought me back empty. Why would you call me Naomi, when the LORD has testified against me, and the Almighty has brought calamity upon me?"

**22** So Naomi returned, and with her Ruth the Moabitess, her daughter-in-law, who returned from the fields of Moab. They came to Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Verses 2, 3, 4, 5, 17 (oath formula), and 22 (basic narrative content) showed strong alignment across all three translators in substance, differing only in stylistic register. Where all three substantially agreed, SOPHIA adopted the formal-equivalent rendering as the center of gravity, preserving Hebrew narrative conventions (waw-consecutive openings, verbal order) while ensuring natural English readability.

The manuscript images (Leningrad Codex, West Semitic Research Project / Bruce Zuckerman photographs) were consulted for all 22 verses. The three-column layout with Masoretic marginal notes is clearly visible. All translators correctly identify the source pages as 880-881. The text of Ruth begins in the right-hand column following the end of Judges, consistent with the Codex's arrangement of the Megillot.

Key term decisions for this chapter:
- **hesed** (1:8): Rendered "faithful love" -- balancing EMET's "lovingkindness," LOGOS's "faithful love," and PNEUMA's "loyal love." "Faithful love" best captures both covenantal loyalty and active kindness.
- **davaq** (1:14): Rendered "clung to" -- all three translators agree on the covenantal weight of this verb (cf. Gen 2:24), though PNEUMA expanded it to "held on to her and would not let go."

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 -- Opening phrase and "fields/country of Moab"
- **EMET:** "And-it-was in-[the]-days of-[the]-judging of-the-judges" / "fields of Moab"
- **LOGOS:** "And it came to pass in the days when the judges judged" / "fields of Moab"
- **PNEUMA:** "Back in the days when judges ruled" / "country of Moab"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "In the days when the judges judged" -- retaining the Hebrew participial construction that EMET and LOGOS preserve, while smoothing EMET's hyphenated literalism. PNEUMA's "Back in the days" is too colloquial for the opening of a biblical narrative. For the geographical term, SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS with "fields of Moab" (Hebrew: sedey Mo'av), as this is the construct form in the Leningrad Codex. PNEUMA's "country of Moab" obscures the agricultural nuance.

### Verse 1 -- "to sojourn" vs. "to live as a foreigner"
- **EMET:** "to-sojourn"
- **LOGOS:** "to sojourn"
- **PNEUMA:** "went to live as a foreigner"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA retains "to sojourn" -- the traditional rendering of lagur, which carries the specific legal status of temporary resident alien. PNEUMA's expansion, while accurate in meaning, loses the concision of the Hebrew.

### Verse 4 -- "the second" vs. "the other"
- **EMET:** "the-second"
- **LOGOS:** "the second"
- **PNEUMA:** "the other"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the other" -- the Hebrew hasshenit can mean either "the second" or "the other." In context, where two women are being named, "the other" reads more naturally in English and avoids implying a hierarchy.

### Verse 6 -- "visited/attended to/turned his attention back to"
- **EMET:** "visited YHWH his-people to-give to-them bread"
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD had attended to His people by giving them bread"
- **PNEUMA:** "the LORD had turned his attention back to his people and given them food"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "attended to" -- preserving the sense of divine visitation (paqad) without the archaic ring of "visited" (EMET) or the interpretive expansion of "turned his attention back to" (PNEUMA). "Bread" is retained over PNEUMA's "food" as it translates lehem literally.

### Verse 7 -- "they went on the way" vs. "they started down the road"
- **EMET:** "and-they-went on-the-way to-return"
- **LOGOS:** "And they went on the way to return"
- **PNEUMA:** "They started down the road that would take them back"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's formal rendering. PNEUMA's expansion ("that would take them back") adds interpretive content not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 8 -- hesed rendering
- **EMET:** "lovingkindness [hesed]"
- **LOGOS:** "faithful love"
- **PNEUMA:** "loyal love"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "faithful love" (LOGOS). All three renderings capture part of hesed's semantic range. "Faithful love" balances covenantal fidelity and relational warmth better than the archaism of "lovingkindness" or the slightly narrower "loyal love."

### Verse 8 -- "each woman to the house of her mother" vs. "both of you -- return to your mothers' homes"
- **EMET:** "each-woman to-[the]-house of-her-mother"
- **LOGOS:** "each woman to the house of her mother"
- **PNEUMA:** "both of you -- return to your mothers' homes"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with the singular distributive "each of you to the house of her mother" -- preserving the Hebrew singular construction (ishah lebeit immah). PNEUMA's pluralization ("mothers' homes") departs from the Hebrew form.

### Verse 9 -- "rest" vs. "security of a new husband's home"
- **EMET:** "find rest, each-woman [in the]-house of-her-husband"
- **LOGOS:** "that you find rest, each woman in the house of her husband"
- **PNEUMA:** "each of you the security of a new husband's home"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew menuha means "rest" or "settled security." PNEUMA's "security of a new husband's home" is interpretive expansion; the word "new" is not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 9 -- "wept" vs. "broke down weeping"
- **EMET:** "they-lifted-up their-voice and-wept"
- **LOGOS:** "they lifted up their voices and wept"
- **PNEUMA:** "they broke down weeping"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew idiom (vatissena qolan vatibkeyna) is a standard biblical expression for vocal mourning. PNEUMA's "broke down weeping," while vivid, loses the formulaic quality.

### Verse 10 -- "No, for with you" vs. "No, but we will" vs. "No! We will"
- **EMET:** "No, for with-you we-will-return to-your-people"
- **LOGOS:** "No, but we will return with you to your people"
- **PNEUMA:** "No! We will go back with you, to your people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. EMET preserves the Hebrew ki ("for/because") but the English reads awkwardly. PNEUMA's exclamation mark adds emotion not marked in the Hebrew text.

### Verse 13 -- "more than you" / "for me than for you" / "My lot is far more bitter than yours"
- **EMET:** "it-is-bitter to-me exceedingly more-than-you"
- **LOGOS:** "it is far more bitter for me than for you"
- **PNEUMA:** "My lot is far more bitter than yours"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew mikkem is ambiguous (see EMET's note), but the comparative sense "for me than for you" is the most natural reading. PNEUMA's "My lot" adds a word not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 14 -- davaq: "clung to" vs. "held on to her and would not let go"
- **EMET:** "Ruth clung [davaq] to-her"
- **LOGOS:** "Ruth clung to her"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth held on to her and would not let go"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "clung to" -- the single verb davaq is best rendered with a single English verb. PNEUMA's double-clause expansion, while evocative, over-translates a one-word Hebrew verb.

### Verse 15 -- "Behold" vs. "Look"
- **EMET:** "Behold"
- **LOGOS:** "Behold"
- **PNEUMA:** "Look"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Look" -- while hinneh is traditionally rendered "behold," the speaker is Naomi in conversation, and "Look" better captures the colloquial immediacy of the Hebrew particle in dialogue. However, this is a close call; "Behold" is equally defensible.

### Verse 16 -- "Do not urge me" vs. "Do not press me" vs. "Don't ask me"
- **EMET:** "Do-not-urge me to-leave-you"
- **LOGOS:** "Do not press me to abandon you"
- **PNEUMA:** "Don't ask me to leave you"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Do not urge me to leave you" -- combining EMET's verb "urge" (closest to Hebrew tipge'i bi, from paga', meaning to press/encounter/urge) with natural phrasing. LOGOS's "abandon" is stronger than the Hebrew "leave" (la'azvekh). PNEUMA's "ask" is too mild for the Hebrew verb.

### Verse 16 -- "your people are / shall be / will be my people"
- **EMET:** "your-people [are] my-people"
- **LOGOS:** "Your people shall be my people"
- **PNEUMA:** "Your people will be my people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "shall be" (LOGOS) -- the Hebrew is verbless (ammekh ammi), which can express present or future reality. "Shall be" captures the volitional, oath-like character of Ruth's declaration better than the simple present (EMET) or the flat future (PNEUMA). For this most celebrated passage, the elevated register of "shall be" is fitting.

### Verse 17 -- Oath formula rendering
- **EMET:** "Thus may-YHWH-do to-me and-thus may-He-add, for [only] death will-separate between-me and-between-you"
- **LOGOS:** "Thus may the LORD do to me, and more besides, if anything but death parts me from you"
- **PNEUMA:** "May the LORD deal with me, and worse, if anything but death separates me from you"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Thus may the LORD do to me, and more besides" best renders the formal oath formula (koh ya'aseh YHWH li vekhoh yosif). PNEUMA's "and worse" is too informal. EMET's "between-me and-between-you" preserves the doubled preposition but reads awkwardly.

### Verse 18 -- "strengthening herself" vs. "determined" vs. "absolutely determined"
- **EMET:** "she-[was]-strengthening-herself to-go"
- **LOGOS:** "she was determined to go"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth was absolutely determined to go"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew mitammetzet (hithpael of 'amatz) means "strengthening herself" or "being resolute." "Determined" captures this well. PNEUMA's "absolutely" is an intensifier not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 19 -- "was stirred" / "was buzzing with excitement" and speakers
- **EMET:** "was-stirred all the-city on-account-of-them, and-they-said"
- **LOGOS:** "the whole city was stirred on account of them, and the women said"
- **PNEUMA:** "the whole town was buzzing with excitement. The women exclaimed"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS for "was stirred" and specifies "the women said" -- the Hebrew verb vattehoM uses a feminine plural form, indicating women are speaking. LOGOS correctly identifies the speakers. PNEUMA's "buzzing with excitement" is interpretive; the Hebrew implies agitation or commotion, not necessarily positive excitement.

### Verse 20 -- "has made bitter to me exceedingly" vs. "has dealt very bitterly with me" vs. "has made my life utterly bitter"
- **EMET:** "the-Almighty has-made-bitter to-me exceedingly"
- **LOGOS:** "the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me"
- **PNEUMA:** "the Almighty has made my life utterly bitter"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition of "my life" is not in the Hebrew. EMET's construction is too literal for the consensus.

### Verse 21 -- "has done evil to me" vs. "has brought calamity upon me" vs. "has brought disaster upon me"
- **EMET:** "the-Almighty has-done-evil to-me"
- **LOGOS:** "the Almighty has brought calamity upon me"
- **PNEUMA:** "the Almighty has brought disaster upon me"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "calamity." The Hebrew hera' li means "has done evil/harm to me." EMET's "done evil" could be misread as moral evil rather than harm. PNEUMA's "disaster" and LOGOS's "calamity" both work; SOPHIA selects "calamity" for its slightly more formal register.

### Verse 22 -- "the Moabitess" vs. "the Moabite"
- **EMET:** "Ruth the-Moabitess"
- **LOGOS:** "Ruth the Moabitess"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth the Moabite"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "Moabitess" -- the Hebrew hammo'aviyyah is a feminine gentilic form, and "Moabitess" preserves this grammatical distinction. The term appears at a structurally significant point, marking Ruth's identity as she enters Bethlehem.

---

*Signed, SOPHIA*
